title: Acute Sinusitis
oneLiner: Acute inflammation of the paranasal sinuses, usually viral and self-limiting; important to identify the minority with bacterial infection and recognise red flags for serious complications.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Orbital cellulitis** – Proptosis, painful ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity, lid oedema.  
  *Single finding*: inability to move the eye fully in any direction.
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis** – Bilateral eye signs, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), high fever, headache, drowsiness.  
  *Single finding*: bilateral periorbital oedema + ptosis.
- **Intracranial abscess** – New focal neurological deficit, seizure, GCS drop, meningism.  
  *Single finding*: progressive headache + vomiting + papilloeedema.
- **Meningitis** – Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental state.  
  *Single finding*: positive Kernig’s sign / Brudzinski’s sign.
- **Pott’s puffy tumour** – Osteomyelitis of the frontal bone; swelling and tenderness over the forehead.  
  *Single finding*: boggy, tender swelling of the forehead (often preceded by sinusitis).
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms (≥2 of the following):
  - Nasal congestion / obstruction
  - Purulent discharge (anterior or post-nasal drip)
  - Facial pain/pressure over affected sinus (maxillary, frontal, ethmoid)
  - Reduced or loss of smell
  - Headache (often worse when bending forward)
- Additional: fever, malaise, toothache (upper teeth), cough (post-nasal drip)
- Key exam findings:
  - Tenderness on palpation over the sinus (maxillary: cheek; frontal: above eyebrow)
  - Nasal examination: swollen erythematous mucosa, purulent secretions
  - Transillumination (if available): dullness over sinus (low sensitivity)
  - Check for eye signs: visual acuity, pupillary responses, eye movements, proptosis, conjunctival injection
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral rhinosinusitis (common cold)** – Duration <10 days, symptoms bilateral, clear or white discharge, no fever >38°C; self-limiting.
- **Allergic rhinitis** – Itchy eyes/nose, sneezing, seasonal triggers, clear discharge, no facial pain.
- **Dental infection** – Unilateral maxillary pain, tooth tenderness, foul taste, normal nasal exam.
- **Migraine** – Unilateral throbbing headache, photophobia, nausea, no nasal signs.
- **Tension headache** – Bilateral band-like pressure, no nasal symptoms.
- **Temporomandibular joint disorder** – Jaw pain, clicking, worse with chewing.
- **Cluster headache** – Unilateral severe orbital pain with lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, ptosis.
- **Splitter:** Duration >10 days with worsening after initial improvement, purulent discharge, and unilateral maxillary tooth pain favours bacterial sinusitis over viral.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Anterior rhinoscopy (with otoscope or nasal speculum). Check transillumination if available. Assess cranial nerves and eye movements. Basic visual acuity (Snellen chart).
- **Bloods:** Routine if uncomplicated and well – unnecessary. If toxic or complications suspected: FBC, CRP, blood cultures.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: No imaging for uncomplicated acute sinusitis** (diagnosis is clinical).
  - **Second-line:** If complication suspected (orbital signs, neurological signs, severe frontal headache): urgent CT sinuses with contrast (or MRI if intracranial involvement).
  - **X-ray sinuses:** not recommended (NICE) – poor sensitivity/specificity.
- **Special tests:** Nasal swab for culture (rarely needed; consider in immunocompromised, refractory cases, or outbreak settings).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE clinical diagnosis – acute rhinosinusitis if symptoms <12 weeks with two of: nasal obstruction, discharge, facial pain/pressure, reduced smell. Suggest bacterial if persistent >10 days with purulent discharge and unilateral maxillary pain, or double-sickening.
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent CT sinuses with contrast** if any red flag for orbital or intracranial complication (proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, visual loss, GCS drop, focal neurology, severe headache with vomiting). Delay can lead to permanent vision loss or death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If no red flags: reassure, discharge with advice.
  - If red flags: start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav, or ceftriaxone+metronidazole if penicillin allergy) and arrange urgent ENT referral + imaging.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Only if admitted for complications or severe systemic illness. Continue IV antibiotics; monitor for neurological deterioration; ENT review daily; consider surgical drainage if no improvement.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** (vast majority)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS, ibuprofen 400mg TDS PRN (if no contraindications).
  - Nasal saline irrigation (e.g., 2-4 times daily) – evidence supports symptom relief.
  - Intranasal corticosteroids: e.g., mometasone furoate 200 mcg each nostril once daily for 14 days (NICE). Can be started at day 10 if symptoms persist.
  - Decongestants (e.g., xylometazoline 0.1% spray): max 7 days – avoid prolonged use.
  - Antibiotics: not first-line. Consider only if:
    - Symptoms >10 days with purulent discharge and unilateral maxillary pain
    - Severe systemic symptoms (fever ≥38°C, severe pain) for >3 days
    - Double-sickening (improvement then worsening)
    - Options: phenoxymethylpenicillin 500mg QDS for 5-7 days; if penicillin allergic: doxycycline 200mg on day 1 then 100mg OD for 5-7 days. Or clarithromycin 500mg BD for 5-7 days.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride / 5% glucose at maintenance rate (e.g., 30 mL/kg/24h). VTE prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin if immobile. Diet as tolerated (NBM only if surgery planned). Observations: 4-hourly including NEWS2.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Amoxicillin:* 500mg TDS PO for 5-7 days (if bacterial) – but NICE suggests no antibiotic or delayed prescription for most.
  - *Phenoxymethylpenicillin:* 500mg-1g QDS PO for 5-7 days (preferred by BNF for acute sinusitis).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *Doxycycline:* 200mg stat then 100mg OD PO for 5-7 days (if penicillin allergic).
  - *Clarithromycin:* 500mg BD PO for 5-7 days (if contraindication to doxycycline, e.g., pregnancy).
  - *Co-amoxiclav:* 625mg TDS PO for 7 days (reserved for severe or resistant cases – seek local guidance).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: avoid in children <12 years, pregnancy/breastfeeding; reduce absorption with dairy/antacids (take 1h before or 2h after).
  - Clarithromycin: strong CYP3A4 inhibitor – check for interacting drugs (statins, warfarin, colchicine, etc.).
  - Amoxicillin: rash in glandular fever; anaphylaxis risk if true allergy.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Amoxicillin: caution if eGFR <10 mL/min – reduce dose/frequency (e.g., 500mg daily).
  - Clarithromycin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (e.g., 250mg BD).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing oral antibiotics without checking for orbital/intracranial complication** – delaying urgent IV therapy worsens outcomes. Also: **prolonged use of nasal decongestant (>7 days)** leads to rhinitis medicamentosa.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Orbital cellulitis / subperiosteal abscess
  - Cavernous sinus thrombosis
  - Intracranial abscess (frontal lobe)
  - Meningitis
  - Pott's puffy tumour (frontal osteomyelitis)
  - Sepsis
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps
  - Mucocele formation
  - Osteomyelitis (rare)
  - Recurrent acute episodes
sections.Prognosis: - Vast majority resolve spontaneously within 2-3 weeks. Only about 2% of acute sinusitis has bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. Risk of complications <0.5%. With appropriate treatment, full recovery expected. Mortality from complications can be 5-20% if untreated.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO give a delayed antibiotic prescription** (patient to use after 7 days if no improvement) – reduces antibiotic use while addressing patient expectation.
- **DO always examine the eyes** (visual acuity, eye movements, pupils, proptosis) – the earliest signs of orbital spread.
- **DO ask about dental pain** – a single upper tooth abscess can mimic maxillary sinusitis and requires dental referral.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NOT giving antibiotics for every case** – 80% are viral; antibiotics cause side effects and resistance. Only prescribe if clear bacterial criteria met.
- **NOT missing orbital signs** – subtle proptosis or mild diplopia can be rapidly progressive. Always check visual acuity and extraocular movements.
- **NOT ordering CT sinuses for uncomplicated sinusitis** – low diagnostic yield, radiation exposure, and delays. Reserve for complications or recurrent disease (>4 episodes/year).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening headache unresponsive to analgesia, especially with vomiting
  - Swelling or redness around the eye, double vision, reduced vision
  - New neck stiffness, photophobia, rash, or altered mental state
  - Fever >39°C with rigors not settling with simple analgesia
- **Follow-up:** Routine – no follow-up needed if symptoms resolve. If persistent >14 days despite first-line treatment, consider GP review or ENT referral. If recurrent (>3 episodes/year), consider chronic rhinosinusitis workup.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ENT immediately if:**
  - Any red flag sign: proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, visual loss, GCS <15, new focal neurology, meningism
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (e.g., respiratory rate >24, oxygen saturations <91%, systolic BP <90, pulse >130)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (if bloods done)
  - Fever >38.5°C with systemic toxicity
- **Call crash team (2222):** GCS drop to <9, airway compromise, seizure, or cardiorespiratory arrest.

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Sinusitis
**One-liner:** Acute inflammation of the paranasal sinuses, usually viral and self-limiting; important to identify the minority with bacterial infection and recognise red flags for serious complications.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Orbital cellulitis** – Proptosis, painful ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity, lid oedema.  
  *Single finding*: inability to move the eye fully in any direction.
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis** – Bilateral eye signs, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), high fever, headache, drowsiness.  
  *Single finding*: bilateral periorbital oedema + ptosis.
- **Intracranial abscess** – New focal neurological deficit, seizure, GCS drop, meningism.  
  *Single finding*: progressive headache + vomiting + papilloeedema.
- **Meningitis** – Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental state.  
  *Single finding*: positive Kernig’s sign / Brudzinski’s sign.
- **Pott’s puffy tumour** – Osteomyelitis of the frontal bone; swelling and tenderness over the forehead.  
  *Single finding*: boggy, tender swelling of the forehead (often preceded by sinusitis).

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms (≥2 of the following):
  - Nasal congestion / obstruction
  - Purulent discharge (anterior or post-nasal drip)
  - Facial pain/pressure over affected sinus (maxillary, frontal, ethmoid)
  - Reduced or loss of smell
  - Headache (often worse when bending forward)
- Additional: fever, malaise, toothache (upper teeth), cough (post-nasal drip)
- Key exam findings:
  - Tenderness on palpation over the sinus (maxillary: cheek; frontal: above eyebrow)
  - Nasal examination: swollen erythematous mucosa, purulent secretions
  - Transillumination (if available): dullness over sinus (low sensitivity)
  - Check for eye signs: visual acuity, pupillary responses, eye movements, proptosis, conjunctival injection

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral rhinosinusitis (common cold)** – Duration <10 days, symptoms bilateral, clear or white discharge, no fever >38°C; self-limiting.
- **Allergic rhinitis** – Itchy eyes/nose, sneezing, seasonal triggers, clear discharge, no facial pain.
- **Dental infection** – Unilateral maxillary pain, tooth tenderness, foul taste, normal nasal exam.
- **Migraine** – Unilateral throbbing headache, photophobia, nausea, no nasal signs.
- **Tension headache** – Bilateral band-like pressure, no nasal symptoms.
- **Temporomandibular joint disorder** – Jaw pain, clicking, worse with chewing.
- **Cluster headache** – Unilateral severe orbital pain with lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, ptosis.
- **Splitter:** Duration >10 days with worsening after initial improvement, purulent discharge, and unilateral maxillary tooth pain favours bacterial sinusitis over viral.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Anterior rhinoscopy (with otoscope or nasal speculum). Check transillumination if available. Assess cranial nerves and eye movements. Basic visual acuity (Snellen chart).
- **Bloods:** Routine if uncomplicated and well – unnecessary. If toxic or complications suspected: FBC, CRP, blood cultures.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: No imaging for uncomplicated acute sinusitis** (diagnosis is clinical).
  - **Second-line:** If complication suspected (orbital signs, neurological signs, severe frontal headache): urgent CT sinuses with contrast (or MRI if intracranial involvement).
  - **X-ray sinuses:** not recommended (NICE) – poor sensitivity/specificity.
- **Special tests:** Nasal swab for culture (rarely needed; consider in immunocompromised, refractory cases, or outbreak settings).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE clinical diagnosis – acute rhinosinusitis if symptoms <12 weeks with two of: nasal obstruction, discharge, facial pain/pressure, reduced smell. Suggest bacterial if persistent >10 days with purulent discharge and unilateral maxillary pain, or double-sickening.
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent CT sinuses with contrast** if any red flag for orbital or intracranial complication (proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, visual loss, GCS drop, focal neurology, severe headache with vomiting). Delay can lead to permanent vision loss or death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If no red flags: reassure, discharge with advice.
  - If red flags: start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav, or ceftriaxone+metronidazole if penicillin allergy) and arrange urgent ENT referral + imaging.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Only if admitted for complications or severe systemic illness. Continue IV antibiotics; monitor for neurological deterioration; ENT review daily; consider surgical drainage if no improvement.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** (vast majority)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS, ibuprofen 400mg TDS PRN (if no contraindications).
  - Nasal saline irrigation (e.g., 2-4 times daily) – evidence supports symptom relief.
  - Intranasal corticosteroids: e.g., mometasone furoate 200 mcg each nostril once daily for 14 days (NICE). Can be started at day 10 if symptoms persist.
  - Decongestants (e.g., xylometazoline 0.1% spray): max 7 days – avoid prolonged use.
  - Antibiotics: not first-line. Consider only if:
    - Symptoms >10 days with purulent discharge and unilateral maxillary pain
    - Severe systemic symptoms (fever ≥38°C, severe pain) for >3 days
    - Double-sickening (improvement then worsening)
    - Options: phenoxymethylpenicillin 500mg QDS for 5-7 days; if penicillin allergic: doxycycline 200mg on day 1 then 100mg OD for 5-7 days. Or clarithromycin 500mg BD for 5-7 days.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride / 5% glucose at maintenance rate (e.g., 30 mL/kg/24h). VTE prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin if immobile. Diet as tolerated (NBM only if surgery planned). Observations: 4-hourly including NEWS2.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Amoxicillin:* 500mg TDS PO for 5-7 days (if bacterial) – but NICE suggests no antibiotic or delayed prescription for most.
  - *Phenoxymethylpenicillin:* 500mg-1g QDS PO for 5-7 days (preferred by BNF for acute sinusitis).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *Doxycycline:* 200mg stat then 100mg OD PO for 5-7 days (if penicillin allergic).
  - *Clarithromycin:* 500mg BD PO for 5-7 days (if contraindication to doxycycline, e.g., pregnancy).
  - *Co-amoxiclav:* 625mg TDS PO for 7 days (reserved for severe or resistant cases – seek local guidance).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: avoid in children <12 years, pregnancy/breastfeeding; reduce absorption with dairy/antacids (take 1h before or 2h after).
  - Clarithromycin: strong CYP3A4 inhibitor – check for interacting drugs (statins, warfarin, colchicine, etc.).
  - Amoxicillin: rash in glandular fever; anaphylaxis risk if true allergy.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Amoxicillin: caution if eGFR <10 mL/min – reduce dose/frequency (e.g., 500mg daily).
  - Clarithromycin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (e.g., 250mg BD).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing oral antibiotics without checking for orbital/intracranial complication** – delaying urgent IV therapy worsens outcomes. Also: **prolonged use of nasal decongestant (>7 days)** leads to rhinitis medicamentosa.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Orbital cellulitis / subperiosteal abscess
  - Cavernous sinus thrombosis
  - Intracranial abscess (frontal lobe)
  - Meningitis
  - Pott's puffy tumour (frontal osteomyelitis)
  - Sepsis
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps
  - Mucocele formation
  - Osteomyelitis (rare)
  - Recurrent acute episodes

### Prognosis
- Vast majority resolve spontaneously within 2-3 weeks. Only about 2% of acute sinusitis has bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. Risk of complications <0.5%. With appropriate treatment, full recovery expected. Mortality from complications can be 5-20% if untreated.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO give a delayed antibiotic prescription** (patient to use after 7 days if no improvement) – reduces antibiotic use while addressing patient expectation.
- **DO always examine the eyes** (visual acuity, eye movements, pupils, proptosis) – the earliest signs of orbital spread.
- **DO ask about dental pain** – a single upper tooth abscess can mimic maxillary sinusitis and requires dental referral.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NOT giving antibiotics for every case** – 80% are viral; antibiotics cause side effects and resistance. Only prescribe if clear bacterial criteria met.
- **NOT missing orbital signs** – subtle proptosis or mild diplopia can be rapidly progressive. Always check visual acuity and extraocular movements.
- **NOT ordering CT sinuses for uncomplicated sinusitis** – low diagnostic yield, radiation exposure, and delays. Reserve for complications or recurrent disease (>4 episodes/year).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening headache unresponsive to analgesia, especially with vomiting
  - Swelling or redness around the eye, double vision, reduced vision
  - New neck stiffness, photophobia, rash, or altered mental state
  - Fever >39°C with rigors not settling with simple analgesia
- **Follow-up:** Routine – no follow-up needed if symptoms resolve. If persistent >14 days despite first-line treatment, consider GP review or ENT referral. If recurrent (>3 episodes/year), consider chronic rhinosinusitis workup.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ENT immediately if:**
  - Any red flag sign: proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, visual loss, GCS <15, new focal neurology, meningism
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (e.g., respiratory rate >24, oxygen saturations <91%, systolic BP <90, pulse >130)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (if bloods done)
  - Fever >38.5°C with systemic toxicity
- **Call crash team (2222):** GCS drop to <9, airway compromise, seizure, or cardiorespiratory arrest.
